title: Lesson 401
transcript: There's just a little bit of terminology that you need to know related to access policy enforcement. So access policy enforcement is exactly what it sounds like. These are the systems that we can use to enforce the access policies that we've come up with. So when we've decided this user should get access to what, how do we actually enforce that? How do we make sure that this user only gets access to what they're supposed to? And here's the two little bits of terminology that you need to know about PDPs versus PEPs. What the heck are these? The names are actually pretty self-explanatory. So a policy decision point, This is the system that's actually making the decisions as to what a user should get access to. And it would be basing these decisions based on things like need to know, least privilege, what the owner has decided a user should get access to. And the PDP, the policy decision point, is the component that's actually making the decisions, which is different from the PEP, which is, again, pretty self-explanatory, the policy enforcement point. This is the system that's actually enforcing the decisions. So whatever the PDP has decided, if the PDP says, yes, this user should be allowed in, then the policy enforcement point will say, yeah, go ahead. You're allowed in. Or if the policy decision point says, this user is not allowed in, then the policy enforcement point would prevent that user from accessing a system. So these can be combined into one system or they can be separate. You can have like a central decision point and then you can have distributed enforcement points around your organization. There's lots of different ways to architect this, but just there you go. That's PDPs versus PEPs.
